title: Add corrective scope guardrail to developer agent steering file
description:
Background: corrective beads (`bead_id` ending in `-corrective` or `-corrective-N`) are auto-generated by the scheduler when a parent bead blocks on a transient failure. Their job is to fix the specific failure so the parent can be retried. They should NOT add unrelated improvements, refactors, or additional fixes the agent happens to notice.

Observed in 2026-05-05 session: merge-conflict bead `B-24db26cb`'s corrective was supposed to add DU conflict handling to `_resolve_bead_state_conflicts`, but it ALSO reapplied a deeper `merge_main_into_branch` fix that had previously been reverted. That second, out-of-scope change introduced new regressions and contributed to a multi-hour merge tangle. The corrective's review approved the work because it solved the immediate blocker; nothing in the agent steering said "don't expand scope."

This bead adds a soft guardrail in the developer agent's steering file. No mechanical enforcement — that is deliberately deferred. If this soft guardrail proves insufficient, a follow-up spec can add `expected_files` inheritance from parent → corrective with a finalize-time scope check.

## Required change

In `templates/agents/developer.md` (the canonical developer agent guardrail template), add a section near the top of the body — before the existing scope/style guidance — explaining the corrective-scope rule. Suggested wording:

```markdown
## Corrective bead scope

If your `bead_id` ends in `-corrective` or `-corrective-<N>` (e.g. `B-abc12345-corrective`, `B-abc12345-test-corrective-2`), you are running as a CORRECTIVE bead. Your scope is **strictly limited to fixing the specific failure that blocked the parent bead**. Do not:

- Add unrelated improvements, refactors, or cleanup, even if you notice them
- Reapply previously-reverted changes (the operator reverted them for a reason)
- Expand the surface area beyond what is needed to unblock the parent
- Touch files outside what the parent bead's `expected_files` / `expected_globs` covered, unless the failure is genuinely in another file and there is no smaller fix

If you notice an unrelated issue while fixing the corrective scope: **file a separate bead** via `takt bead create` describing what you saw, but do not fix it in this corrective. The operator will prioritize and dispatch.

When in doubt about scope: prefer the smallest possible fix. The reviewer of this corrective will check that the scope was respected.
```

The exact placement and phrasing can be tightened during implementation; the substance is what matters.

## Acceptance criteria

1. `templates/agents/developer.md` has a "Corrective bead scope" section (or equivalent heading) covering the four "do not" bullets above plus the "file a separate bead" guidance.
2. The section is placed prominently — near the top of the body, before generic implementation guidance — so agents loading the template see it before they start work.
3. No code changes outside `templates/agents/developer.md`. This is a steering-file-only change.
4. `uv run pytest tests/ -n auto -q` passes (no regressions; the change is to a markdown template, no test impact expected).
5. Other agent templates (tester.md, documentation.md, review.md, planner.md, recovery.md) are NOT modified — correctives are always developer-typed in current takt design.

## Out of scope

- Mechanical enforcement (`expected_files` inheritance, finalize-time scope checks). Reserved for a follow-up if this soft guardrail proves insufficient in real usage.
- Updating CLAUDE.md or other operator docs. The change here is for the AGENT, not the operator.
- Changing how the scheduler creates corrective beads.
- Renaming or restructuring the existing `templates/agents/` files.

## References

- `templates/agents/developer.md` — the file to modify
- CLAUDE.md "Corrective beads" subsection in Key Concepts (background on how correctives are created)
- Session lesson from 2026-05-05: merge-conflict bead `B-24db26cb`'s corrective expanded scope and broke the merge — the case study that motivated this bead

handoff_summary.completed: Added corrective-scope guardrail section to `templates/agents/developer.md`. Section placed at the top of the body (lines 5–16) so it is the first substantive content agents read after the primary responsibility statement.
handoff_summary.remaining: N/A — bead is complete. Mechanical enforcement (expected_files inheritance, finalize-time scope checks) is explicitly deferred to a follow-up spec if this soft guardrail proves insufficient.
handoff_summary.risks: None. This is a markdown template change with no impact on executable code paths or tests.
handoff_summary.next_action: Review the steering-file change and merge if accepted.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: None — only `templates/agents/developer.md` was modified; no sibling beads are likely to touch this file.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: Placed the section immediately after the primary responsibility line and before the Allowed/Disallowed actions block. This ensures corrective agents encounter the scope constraint before any other guidance, maximizing the chance they read it before beginning work. The wording follows the spec's suggested text closely, with minor tightening for flow. No alternative placements (e.g., at end of file, inside Disallowed actions) were used because prominence was an explicit acceptance criterion.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: N/A — this bead touched only a markdown template file. No executable code paths were modified; no test coverage is needed or applicable.
handoff_summary.known_limitations: This is a soft guardrail only — there is no mechanical enforcement preventing a corrective agent from ignoring it. A follow-up spec could add expected_files inheritance from parent → corrective with a finalize-time scope check if real-world usage shows the soft guardrail is insufficient.

conflict_risks: None — only `templates/agents/developer.md` was modified; no sibling beads are likely to touch this file.

execution_history.summary: Added a "Corrective bead scope" section to `templates/agents/developer.md`. The section is placed immediately after the primary responsibility line (before Allowed/Disallowed actions), covering all four "do not" bullets, the "file a separate bead" guidance, and the "smallest possible fix" principle. No other files were modified.

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `{{LANGUAGE}}` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: `{{BUILD_CHECK_COMMAND}}`.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `{{TEST_COMMAND}}` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

## Memory

**Read memory at bead start.** Before touching any code, run three searches using `$TAKT_CMD` (injected by the orchestrator):

```bash
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace global
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace feature:<feature_root_id>
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace specs
```

Treat results as ambient context — apply relevant entries; skip entries that don't apply.

**Write to memory when you discover reusable project knowledge** — something that would have changed your approach if you had known it upfront, and is not already in CLAUDE.md or the guardrails.

```bash
$TAKT_CMD memory add "<concise fact>" --namespace global               # project-wide knowledge
$TAKT_CMD memory add "<discovery>" --namespace feature:<feature_root_id>  # feature-scoped
```

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
